title: Emmerson Parsons Table
summary: A modern Parsons-style dining table with a scrap-wood tabletop and a chunky look built from a hidden 2x2 frame wrapped in 1x3 and 1x2 trim.
description:
This table's chunky, reclaimed-wood look is a bit of a trick — underneath the visible legs and aprons is a plain 2x2 structural frame, entirely wrapped in 1x3 and 1x2 trim boards. That two-layer approach is what lets you use inexpensive 2x2 stock for the actual joinery while still getting the substantial look of thick legs, and it means the trim boards can be a different, more attractive species than the hidden frame.

The tabletop itself is built from scrap wood of matching thickness, glued and nailed down with the board joints deliberately landing on top of a 2x2 support rather than spanning empty space — that's what keeps a scrap-wood top from flexing or creaking at the seams. One fitting detail worth planning for: a standard leg here measures 2-1/4" wide, but a 1x3 is 2-1/2" wide, so covering the legs with 1x3 as-is leaves a 1/4" overhang. Most people find that barely noticeable, but ripping the 1x3 down to 2-1/4" first gives a perfectly flush wrap if that overhang bothers you.

tools.note: Trims the scrap tabletop boards to fit and can rip the 1x3 trim down to 2-1/4" for a flush leg wrap.

materials.name: 1x3 board, for covering legs and aprons (at least 24 ft total)
1x2 board, for covering aprons (at least 10 ft total)
Scrap wood, matching thickness, for the tabletop
materials.note: Reclaimed or offcut boards work well here — this is the piece the table is named for.

cutList.note: Two join the leg sets directly; the rest are extra supports under the tabletop.
Cut to fit around each leg and apron face as you wrap them; rip to 2-1/4" wide first for a flush fit over the 2-1/4" legs.

steps.title: Build the leg sets
steps.body: Attach two of the 25" 2x2 pieces to the legs to create two leg sets — one 25" piece per set, joining a pair of legs. Use 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws.

steps.title: Attach the side aprons
steps.body: Attach the side aprons to the two leg sets, alternating which side the pocket holes are drilled on from one joint to the next — this keeps you from clustering too many pocket holes into the same spot on a leg. Use glue.

steps.title: Add the under-table supports
steps.body: Attach the remaining 25" 2x2 supports to the side aprons with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws. Add extra supports beyond what's listed if you're using shorter scrap pieces for the tabletop — every seam in the top needs a support underneath it.

steps.title: Build the scrap-wood tabletop
steps.body: Use glue and 1-1/4" finish nails to attach the scrap wood boards to the top of the frame. Make sure every board joint lands directly on top of a 2x2 support underneath. You'll likely need to trim some board edges with a circular saw or table saw to get everything to fit together well.
materials: Scrap wood, matching thickness, for the tabletop

steps.title: Cover the legs and aprons with trim
steps.body: Wrap the legs and aprons — both the ends and the sides — with the 1x3 and 1x2 trim boards, mitering the corners where they meet. A leg measures 2-1/4" wide, while a 1x3 is 2-1/2" wide; using the 1x3 as-is leaves a 1/4" overhang that's barely noticeable, or rip it down to 2-1/4" first for a perfectly flush wrap. Pocket-hole the corners generously — this trim is what gives the legs their visible strength and look, so it needs to be solidly attached, not just decorative.
materials: 1x3 board, for covering legs and aprons (at least 24 ft total)
1x2 board, for covering aprons (at least 10 ft total)

images.alt: Modern Parsons dining table with a scrap-wood top and wrapped 1x3 legs
